currentQuizCards.name: King of Wands
currentQuizCards.upright: The King of Wands sits forward on his throne, salamanders and lions on his robe, a wand in hand. He is the visionary leader — someone who inspires others with a genuine sense of where things are going and the authority to get there. In a reading he represents entrepreneurial drive, bold vision, and the mature expression of creative fire.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Arrogance, tyranny, vision without listening, or energy that burns out those around it.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Emperor: vision meeting structure. Next to The Chariot: unstoppable creative drive. Next to The High Priestess: the leader who needs to listen before acting.

scenario.love: A passionate, decisive partner with strong presence — which can be exciting or overwhelming.
scenario.work: Entrepreneurial leadership, a visionary project, or the moment to step into a larger role.
scenario.self: Are you leading from genuine vision or from the need to be in charge?

currentQuizCards.name: King of Cups
currentQuizCards.upright: The King of Cups sits on a throne amid choppy seas, calm and composed. He has mastered emotion not by suppressing it but by integrating it — he feels fully and leads wisely. In a reading he represents emotional maturity, the capacity to hold space for others without being destabilised, and the authority that comes from genuine self-knowledge.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Emotional manipulation, volatility beneath a calm surface, or emotional intelligence used to control.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Emperor: power held with both feeling and structure. Next to The Hermit: wisdom earned through solitude. Next to The Devil: emotional mastery turned to manipulation.

scenario.love: A mature, emotionally available partner. Or the call to bring that quality to your own relationships.
scenario.work: Leadership that holds the emotional temperature of a team with steadiness and care.
scenario.self: Where are you being called to lead with feeling rather than just authority?

currentQuizCards.name: Knight of Swords
currentQuizCards.upright: The Knight of Swords charges at full gallop, sword raised, into the wind. He is the fastest knight — direct, ambitious, cutting through with words and action. He does not pause. In a reading he can represent someone with this quality, or the call to act decisively and communicate clearly — with the warning that speed without strategy creates its own problems.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Recklessness, aggression, using sharpness to wound rather than to clarify.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Chariot: drive that is also focused. Next to The Tower: speed creating destruction. Next to The Eight of Wands: everything moving very fast.

scenario.love: Direct, possibly blunt communication about where things stand.
scenario.work: Decisive action in a fast-moving situation — or someone who steamrolls rather than leads.
scenario.self: Where does your decisiveness serve you? And where does it get ahead of everyone else?

currentQuizCards.name: Page of Wands
currentQuizCards.upright: The Page of Wands stands in a desert, wand in hand, full of energy and curiosity. He has not yet done anything — but he wants to. This card speaks to the enthusiasm of the beginner, the excitement of new ideas before they encounter obstacles. In a reading it can signal a new creative direction, a message about a project, or the invitation to approach something with fresh energy.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Creative blocks, immaturity, energy without direction.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Ace of Wands: the spark becoming a person. Next to The Fool: pure beginning energy. Next to The Seven of Cups: enthusiasm that needs grounding.

scenario.love: New romantic energy, or the return of excitement in an existing relationship.
scenario.work: A new creative or entrepreneurial direction with genuine fire behind it.
scenario.self: Where are you approaching something with beginner's mind — and where have you stopped?

currentQuizCards.name: Knight of Pentacles
currentQuizCards.upright: The Knight of Pentacles sits on a heavy horse, unmoving, a pentacle in hand. He is the only knight not charging. He is methodical, dependable, and committed to completing what he starts. In a reading he represents the slow but certain approach — the person who may not be exciting but absolutely delivers. He asks whether this situation calls for patience and thoroughness rather than speed.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Stubbornness, getting stuck in routine, or so much caution that nothing moves.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Chariot: steady pace versus full speed — which is right? Next to The Eight of Pentacles: patient skill development. Next to The Seven of Pentacles: the investment that will pay off.

scenario.love: A reliable, steady partner who shows up consistently — less dramatic than some, more sustaining.
scenario.work: Methodical execution of a long plan. The tortoise, not the hare.
scenario.self: Where does your reliability serve you, and where does caution become inertia?

currentQuizCards.name: Page of Swords
currentQuizCards.upright: The Page of Swords stands on a hilltop, sword raised, alert to everything around them. This is the mind at its most curious and watchful — quick, perceptive, always scanning. In a reading the Page of Swords can represent a message of importance, a young person with a sharp mind, or the quality of alert intelligence that a situation requires.
currentQuizCards.reversed: Gossip, using intelligence to wound, or vigilance that has become paranoia.
currentQuizCards.relational: Next to The Ace of Swords: a sharp mind finding its focus. Next to The Seven of Swords: intelligence used deceptively. Next to The High Priestess: curiosity that could become genuine wisdom.

scenario.love: A perceptive, sharp-minded person entering the picture — or the need for more honest communication.
scenario.work: The early stages of research, analysis, or a situation requiring sharp observation.
scenario.self: What are you watching, and what are you not letting yourself see?

spread.name: Three Card
spread.description: Past, Present, Future — the spine of any reading.

card.name: The Chariot
card.upright: Two sphinxes pull the chariot in opposite directions — one black, one white. The Charioteer holds no reins. He controls them through pure will and focused intention. This is the card of hard-won forward movement, of harnessing opposing forces and driving them toward a goal. Ambition, determination, and the refusal to be pulled apart.
card.reversed: Loss of direction, scattered energy, a vehicle with no driver. Reversed, The Chariot can also signal aggression used as a substitute for clarity.
card.relational: Next to Strength: willpower meeting patience — powerful. Next to The Moon: direction lost in confusion. Next to The Wheel: momentum working with or against you.

scenario.love: A relationship moving forward despite obstacles — or a person who is hard to keep up with.
scenario.work: Drive and ambition in full force. The ability to push through opposition.
scenario.self: What opposing forces in yourself are you trying to reconcile, and are you doing it with force or with mastery?

card.name: King of Wands
card.upright: The King of Wands sits forward on his throne, salamanders and lions on his robe, a wand in hand. He is the visionary leader — someone who inspires others with a genuine sense of where things are going and the authority to get there. In a reading he represents entrepreneurial drive, bold vision, and the mature expression of creative fire.
card.reversed: Arrogance, tyranny, vision without listening, or energy that burns out those around it.
card.relational: Next to The Emperor: vision meeting structure. Next to The Chariot: unstoppable creative drive. Next to The High Priestess: the leader who needs to listen before acting.

scenario.love: A passionate, decisive partner with strong presence — which can be exciting or overwhelming.
scenario.work: Entrepreneurial leadership, a visionary project, or the moment to step into a larger role.
scenario.self: Are you leading from genuine vision or from the need to be in charge?

He is methodical, dependable, and committed to completing what he starts. In a reading he represents the slow but certain approach — the person who may not be exciting but absolutely delivers. He asks whether this situation calls for patience and thoroughness rather than speed.

card.relational: Next to The Chariot: steady pace versus full speed — which is right? Next to The Eight of Pentacles: patient skill development. Next to The Seven of Pentacles: the investment that will pay off.

scenario.work: Methodical execution of a long plan. The tortoise, not the hare.

currentReading.querrant: A client has recently been made redundant after 12 years at the same company. They feel lost.

steps.prompt: You look at the spread. The Hierophant sits in the Past position. Before addressing the redundancy directly, what does this card tell you about how they experienced that 12-year period?

options.text: They valued the structure and identity that the institution gave them — perhaps too much.
options.chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

options.text: They were probably too conventional in how they worked — never challenged the rules.
options.chosen_response: That's a possible reading, but it's evaluative rather than curious. The Hierophant in past position more usefully asks: what did that structure mean to them? Judgement about whether they should have challenged it comes later, if at all.

options.text: Religious or cultural values shaped their career choices.
options.chosen_response: Possible, but you're narrowing too fast. The Hierophant's institutional dimension is broader than religion. The more useful question at this stage is: how much of their identity was tied to belonging to that particular institution?

steps._chosen_response: Yes. The Hierophant in the past position often signals that the querent found significant meaning in belonging to a structure. 12 years at one company, especially if that company had strong culture or hierarchy, can become an identity. The first thing worth acknowledging is that what was lost wasn't just a job.

steps.prompt: Death sits in the Present. How do you introduce this card to someone who is already frightened?

options.text: Immediately reassure them that Death never means actual death, then explain the transformation meaning.
options.chosen_response: The reassurance impulse is understandable, but leading with it actually increases anxiety — it signals there's something to be scared of. A better approach: name what is true. Something has ended. That is real, and it matters. Then open toward what becomes possible when something ends.

options.text: Acknowledge that something genuinely has ended, and that grief is the right response — then ask what they are still trying to hold onto.
options.chosen_response: Exactly. Death in the present isn't a threat — it's a description. Something has ended. The grief is real and worth honouring. But the productive question for a reading is: what are they still trying to sustain that has already gone? That's where the actual work lives.

options.text: Focus on transformation — this is a new beginning in disguise.
options.chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps._chosen_response: You're jumping to the hopeful frame too quickly. Transformation is part of the card's meaning, but if you go straight there you skip the acknowledgement of loss that the querent actually needs to hear first. The sequence matters.

steps.prompt: The Star closes the spread in the Future position. After what you've said about the Hierophant and Death, how do you read this?

options.text: Everything will be fine — the Star is a positive card and the future looks good.
options.chosen_response: Too easy. The Star is genuinely hopeful but it isn't a promise of outcome — it's a quality of possibility. Given that the Past shows deep institutional identity and the Present shows real loss, the Star in the future position is asking: can you let yourself hope for something that is genuinely yours, rather than something given to you by an institution?

options.text: Recovery and renewal are available — but the sequence suggests it requires genuinely releasing what's gone, not just waiting it out.
options.chosen_response: Good. The narrative arc across these three cards is: deep institutional belonging → real ending → the possibility of something more authentically one's own. The Star is available, but it follows Death. You can't rush the middle card.

options.text: They should look for work in a field that feels more meaningful and personally chosen.
options.chosen_response: That might be true but you've shifted from reading the cards to giving career advice. The Star speaks to the inner state that makes what comes next possible — renewed hope, trust in the world, a less defended openness. Stay with that.

currentQuizFeedback.good: Well spotted. Red — Desire, action, and the life force — the colour of passion and of blood. Red garments mark active engagement with the world.
currentQuizFeedback.bad: Look again — the card carries this one: Red — Desire, action, and the life force — the colour of passion and of blood. Red garments mark active engagement with the world.

profile.title: The Initiated Reader
profile.summary: Your instincts are already moving beyond surface symbolism. You understand that position shapes meaning, that cards in combination create narrative, and that the useful question is rarely what a card "means" in isolation.
profile.startPoint: Begin directly with spread practice. The Minor Arcana will be your focus — you don't need the Major Arcana foundations.
